underload_justification.explain_why_you_need_an_underload_for_th:
I am requesting permission to register for an underload (30 units instead of the usual 36+) for Spring 2025 due to an ongoing combination of medical and personal circumstances that have significantly affected my ability to sustain a full Caltech course load.

Medical context: I have been under the care of Student Wellness Services and an outside neurologist for chronic migraine with aura since my first year, but the condition has worsened noticeably over the past two terms. I currently experience 3–4 migraine days per week, often triggered by extended screen time, fluorescent lighting in classrooms, and irregular sleep. During a migraine episode, I have difficulty reading, focusing, and sometimes even standing up without dizziness. This has already led to multiple missed lectures, problem set sessions, and office hours this year, and I have had to request several extensions from my instructors.

In Fall 2024, I attempted to manage a 45-unit load (CS 24, Ma 2, Ph 2, a humanities course, and PE), and I ended the term feeling completely burned out. My grades were acceptable overall, but only because I constantly worked late into the night and sacrificed sleep, which in turn exacerbated the migraines. Over winter break, my neurologist adjusted my medications and strongly recommended reducing my overall workload and especially my weekly screen time. I have documentation on file with Caltech Accessibility Services for testing accommodations, and I am in the process of updating that documentation to reflect the change in my medical status.

Academic/planning context: I am currently a junior (class of 2026) in the Computer Science option and am on track to graduate on time if I plan my next three terms carefully. After meeting with my advisor, Dr. Michael Johnson, we mapped out a sequence of required CS and math courses that will allow me to take 30 units this spring, 30 units in Fall 2025, and 30 units in Winter 2026 while still completing my option requirements and sufficient humanities and PE. I have already completed most of my core requirements, and I am focusing now on upper-level CS courses and a few remaining humanities.

Reason this term is particularly difficult: This spring, CS 24 and Ma 2 both involve heavy weekly problem sets, programming assignments, and long hours in front of a computer. The humanities course I have chosen (Hum 10) has a substantial reading and writing load but is more flexible in terms of pacing and screen time (I can do a lot of the reading on paper and write drafts by hand before typing them up). If I were to add an additional 6–9 unit technical course to reach a full load, I would almost certainly end up back in the same cycle of sleep deprivation and increased migraines that I experienced last fall. My neurologist explicitly advised me not to exceed about 30 hours per week of sustained high-intensity cognitive work and screen use, and my experience at Caltech is that a 45-unit load translates into significantly more than that.

Impact on daily life and performance: On bad migraine days, I may spend several hours lying in a dark room with an ice pack, unable to look at a screen or read. This makes it extremely difficult to keep up with dense technical material that builds from lecture to lecture. I often find myself re-watching recorded lectures at half speed, which doubles the time I need to invest. There have been weeks when I have only been able to attend 50–60% of my scheduled classes in person. The underload would give me a buffer so that when I inevitably lose productivity to migraine days, I am not immediately at risk of falling irreparably behind in multiple high-stakes courses at once.

Support and planning: I have already met with my advisor to discuss this request, and he is supportive of the underload as a way to stabilize my health while staying on track academically. I have also talked with the course instructors for CS 24 and Ma 2 about my situation and about how to manage extensions and exam accommodations in coordination with Accessibility Services. Additionally, I am working with a therapist through Student Wellness Services on stress management strategies, sleep hygiene, and setting realistic limits on my workload. Part of that plan is explicitly not overloading my schedule while I am still adjusting to new medications.

Financial and visa status: I am a domestic student and not on a student visa, so there are no international student status implications to this underload. I have checked with the Financial Aid Office, and at 30 units I will still meet the minimum enrollment requirements for my aid package, provided that this underload is formally approved. I am not an NCAA athlete, so there are no athletic eligibility issues.

Long-term goals and why this is temporary: My long-term goal is to continue in the CS option, complete an undergraduate research project in systems or networking, and eventually apply to graduate school. Taking a slightly lighter load for this specific term (and planning for balanced 30-unit loads in the next two terms) will help me recover some stability, learn to manage my migraines more effectively, and avoid the pattern of pushing through until I crash. I view this underload as a proactive, temporary adjustment that will allow me to stay at Caltech and complete my degree successfully, rather than as a step toward reducing my overall academic engagement.

In summary, I am requesting an underload for Spring 2025 so that I can:
- Keep my total units at a level that is compatible with my current medical limitations.
- Focus on doing well in a smaller set of courses (CS 24, Ma 2, Hum 10, and one PE) instead of spreading myself too thin.
- Follow the medical advice of my neurologist and the recommendations of Student Wellness Services.
- Maintain steady progress toward graduation without risking serious deterioration in my health.

I understand that an underload is an exception to standard policy, and I do not make this request lightly. I am committed to using the additional time and flexibility to manage my health responsibly and to stay fully engaged in the courses I do take.
